summary: hpsetup fetches and installs unverified tarballs from a non-publisher domain, persists itself in CI, and silently grants build-script trust to an unrelated package
details: When invoked (e.g., `npx hpsetup <key>`), this CLI fetches tarballs from `https://hpsetup-cdn.932324.xyz/api/tarball/<slug>/<version>?key=<userKey>` and extracts them into the consumer's `node_modules/@heroui-pro/react` and `heroui-native-pro` directories with no hash or signature verification. The `932324.xyz` host does not match HeroUI's real publisher domain (`heroui.com`); whoever controls that proxy can ship arbitrary JavaScript that subsequently executes whenever the consumer app imports the placed package. After extraction, `dist/postinstall` and any `scripts.postinstall` entry are stripped from the downloaded package.json (src/download.js:11-19), removing forensic traces of what the proxy served. The user's license key is appended as a query parameter (`?key=<userKey>`) on every fetch (src/download.js:24), relaying it to the third-party domain rather than to HeroUI directly. configureVercel writes `vercel.json` with `installCommand: "<dlx> hpsetup@latest <userKey>"` and appends `vercel.json` to the consumer's `.gitignore` (src/vercel.js:34-38) — concealing the change from code review and ensuring every Vercel CI build re-fetches proxy-served code with the embedded key. Finally, src/trust.js:5 hardcodes `@zowe/secrets-for-zowe-sdk` alongside the `@heroui-pro/*` packages into pnpm `onlyBuiltDependencies` / pnpm-workspace `allowBuilds` / bun `trustedDependencies`, silently granting build-script execution privileges to an unrelated Zowe SDK secrets module in the consumer's project.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
